text: The oncological surgery of the stomach is based on one of the fundamental principles, as in part for all oncological surgery. Already at the beginning of the nineteenth century, Lord Moynihan had established that the surgery of the malignant pathology is not an organ surgery, but rather a lymphatic system surgery, which must replace the lymphatic system. We have based all our work on these capsules in the surgery of the neoplastics and in the surgery of the stomach we focused on following what was said by the Japanese school, exactly by Maruyama, that it should be performed at least, to perform a good stadium, a DII lymphadenatomy. The effort that was made in the course of the years, from the beginning of the year, was to try to standardize this more and more and try to obtain the same results with a sufficient number of lymph nodes, at least over forty, similar to those of open surgery, trying to solve all these gaps and difficulties that laparoscopic surgery, being a rigid surgery, has at the expense of open-air surgery.

The surgeon positions four trocars in a rhombus configuration for the gastrectomy procedure: one below the xyphoid, one umbilical, and two lateral trocars, each 12 millimeters in size, to accommodate mechanical sutures. The peritoneum is accessed using a Veress needle in the left hypochondriac region.

The surgeon carefully opens the coloepiploid ligament, avoiding any section of the mesocolon to prevent potential ischemic injury to the transverse colon. The slow and precise dissection reveals the basic average codes. The gastro-global vein on the right is isolated, corresponding to lymph node stations fourteen and six, and the diploid vein on the right is gradually released from the medial colic vessels.

The surgeon carefully dissects around station number fourteen and station number six, revealing the lower image of the pancreas. The L-tronco gradually appears as the gastroepiploic vein on the right is isolated and clipped. The dissection continues with the opening of the gastrocolic ligament and the epiploic neck, completing the omentotomy. The right gastroepiploic artery is dissected at its origin, with the duodenal gastro emerging from the pancreas.

The surgeon removes the lacini to begin the dissection of the gallbladder. The gallbladder is excised to prevent postoperative cholecystitis due to vagus nerve sectioning. The peritoneum on the lower surface of the liver is opened to perform a lymphadenectomy of station XII. The dissection continues with the opening of the flaccid pars and sectioning of vagus nerve branches near the hepatic hilum. The left hepatic artery is dissected first, followed by the tortuous right hepatic artery, located immediately above the renal hilum.

The surgeon completes the posterior dissection, isolating and clipping the pyloric or gastric artery on the right before sectioning it. This facilitates the lymphadenectomy of station five and the completion of station twelve P, located posterior to the Venaporta. Using an ultrasound dissector, the surgeon carefully dissects to avoid periapical and abdental lesions. The ultrasonic dissection of the gallbladder bed reveals the anatomy of the main biliary tract, including the common bile duct and cystic artery originating from the right hepatic artery. The cystic artery is ligated and sectioned, allowing the isolation of the gallbladder below its infundibulum. The gallbladder is dissected and released using a loop, completing the dissection of station twelve.

The surgeon completes the liberation of the right hepatic artery and dissects small lymph nodes leading to the common biliary duct. Using ultrasound detectors, the posterior dissection is carefully performed, freeing the common bile duct up to the pancreatic bulge. The biliary vein dissection is completed with the transportation of station XIII, represented by a single lymph node, which is exported for histological examination. The lymphadenectomy phase exposes the pancreatic and duodenal artery at the hepatic emergency, allowing for the duodenal section.

The surgeon performs an accurate duodenal section, ensuring preservation of the first arterial vessel originating from the right gastroepiploic artery to maintain vascularization. The dissection progresses to expose the left gastric vein, which serves as an anatomical landmark for identifying the celiac triplet. The surgeon completes the dissection of station eight (common hepatic), station seven (left gastric artery), and station nine (celiac triplet), transporting them in a block. The right pillar of the esophageal hiatus of the diaphragm is exposed, and the flaccid pass is opened, with challenges noted due to redundant pancreatic tissue.

The surgeon dissects and sections the first periaortic lymph nodes, completing the right part of the lymphadenectomy. The procedure continues with the left part of the lymphadenectomy, involving the opening of the epiploic neck ligament. The tumor, presenting an inflammatory lesion, is closely associated with the splenic artery. Precise dissection reveals the posterior gastric artery, which is preserved to maintain gastric mucosal vascularization. The sectioning of short gastric vessels provides partial access to the splenic hilum.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the short gastric vessels, revealing the posterior aspect of the spleen and exposing the anterior margin of the left crus of the diaphragm's esophageal hiatus. The esophagus is released from anterior and posterior adhesions, with a length of seven to eight centimeters prepared for anastomosis. The anterior (left) and posterior (right) vagus nerves are carefully dissected and sectioned, enhancing esophageal mobility and facilitating downward traction.

The surgeon transitions to the reconstructive phase, marking the biliary limb with a blue dot and the alimentary limb with a white dot. The section of veins is performed using vascular sutures for precise hemostasis. The anastomosis of the Ileal loop is completed with a running suture using self-locking thread. The transverse mesocolon is opened at a vascular point, and the trans-mesocolic anastomosis is positioned laterally to the left of the median colic, followed by a lateral-lateral anastomosis of the second loop.

The surgeon performs an undercut of the tendon to prevent mucosal flaking, followed by a suture using a 45mm blue stapler. The stomach is positioned in an anti-Trendelenburg orientation to draw the esophagus into the abdomen, facilitating an intra-mediastinal anastomosis. A full-thickness suture is completed, emphasizing the importance of securing the mucosa. A second reinforcement suture is applied using a continuous autostatic technique, guided by a trans-anastomotic probe. Methylene blue is used to check for leaks in the anastomosis, confirming its integrity. The duodenal lance is fixed to prevent passage, and a subhepatic drainage is positioned for 48 hours. The final result shows the placement of trocars and the surgical specimens, including the stomach, station thirteen, and the gallbladder.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the omentum and begins the dissection of lymph node station number twelve, which is removed and sent separately. The small gastric curve is sectioned, releasing station two, which is also sent separately. The stomach is rotated to release station four, including sub-stations four A and four B, and station fourteen, which are sent separately. The dissection continues with station six on the large gastric curve, followed by stations five, eight, seven, and nine, which are removed as a single piece. The disassembly of stations ten and eleven is performed, concluding with stations three and one, which are sent separately due to their potential for metastasis.

The surgeon opens the operating field, revealing a large neoplastic lesion located on the small gastric curve after completing the dissection and freeing the stomach from surrounding lymphatic stations.
